title: Log, show and graph

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Log, show and graph; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Log traverses commit history and references, while show inspects one selected object's metadata and patch; filters narrow evidence without changing history.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A learner searches log for changes to parser.ts, selects a full commit identity, and uses show to inspect that commit's exact patch. Your goal is to locate and explain the commit that introduced a supplied change. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Selective staging and first commit; Commit messages and scope. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: Log traverses commit history and references, while show inspects one selected object's metadata and patch; filters narrow evidence without changing history. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Default history can omit unreachable commits, merges have multiple parents, path filtering changes traversal, and abbreviated hashes can become ambiguous. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: The newest log entry is always the commit that introduced a particular line or behavior.

includes: Inspect commits, parents, authorship, patches and branch topology.
Locate and explain the commit that introduced a supplied change.
excludes: Default history can omit unreachable commits, merges have multiple parents, path filtering changes traversal, and abbreviated hashes can become ambiguous.

outcomes: Locate and explain the commit that introduced a supplied change.

examples.title: Log, show and graph: direct case
examples.situation: A learner searches log for changes to parser.ts, selects a full commit identity, and uses show to inspect that commit's exact patch.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A learner searches log for changes to parser.ts, selects a full commit identity, and uses show to inspect that commit's exact patch.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Use relevant path, author, text, graph, or date filters and inspect candidate commits directly instead of inferring origin from recency alone.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Use relevant path, author, text, graph, or date filters and inspect candidate commits directly instead of inferring origin from recency alone.

examples.title: Log, show and graph: boundary case
examples.situation: A graph view reveals that a merge commit joins two parent lines rather than representing an ordinary single-parent step.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A graph view reveals that a merge commit joins two parent lines rather than representing an ordinary single-parent step.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Default history can omit unreachable commits, merges have multiple parents, path filtering changes traversal, and abbreviated hashes can become ambiguous.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: The newest log entry is always the commit that introduced a particular line or behavior.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Locate and explain the commit that introduced a supplied change.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: The newest log entry is always the commit that introduced a particular line or behavior.
misconceptions.correction: Use relevant path, author, text, graph, or date filters and inspect candidate commits directly instead of inferring origin from recency alone.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: The newest log entry is always the commit that introduced a particular line or behavior.

analogy.example: A signed lab notebook can model Log, show and graph: stable entries record why a change happened, named markers identify milestones, and comparisons expose differences.
usefulFor: Inspecting immutable records, messages, selected versions, differences, and milestone names
limitations: Git history is a directed graph with movable references, merges, and content-addressed objects, not a single bound notebook.
Default history can omit unreachable commits, merges have multiple parents, path filtering changes traversal, and abbreviated hashes can become ambiguous.

recap.summary: You should now be able to locate and explain the commit that introduced a supplied change. Keep this boundary in view: Default history can omit unreachable commits, merges have multiple parents, path filtering changes traversal, and abbreviated hashes can become ambiguous.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Log, show and graph without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: The newest log entry is always the commit that introduced a particular line or behavior.
Apply the model to this case from memory: A graph view reveals that a merge commit joins two parent lines rather than representing an ordinary single-parent step.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Diffs and patch review”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
